critical_failures: Handles POST/REQUEST data without nonce verification

Security: Nonce check relies on a pre-computed global $valid_nonce instead of calling wp_verify_nonce() directly in the handler — this is not a safe pattern and bypasses the intent of nonce verification (the check may have been done in a different context or not at all by the time this function runs).
Security: No capability check (current_user_can()) before processing POST data and modifying config files — any logged-in user could trigger this handler.

Security: $cache_scheduled_time from $_POST is assigned to a variable and passed to strtotime() without sanitization beyond a basic regex that is too loose (preg_match '/[0-9][0-9]:[0-9][0-9]/' does not anchor the pattern, allowing additional characters).
Code Quality: Function is missing a closing brace in the extracted snippet (possible truncation), but the overall structure is deeply nested with multiple responsibilities.
WPCS: Non-Yoda conditions used throughout (e.g., '$_POST["action"] == "expirytime"' instead of '"expirytime" == $_POST["action"]').
WPCS: Inconsistent spacing around array keys and control structures.
Code Quality: Relies entirely on global state ($valid_nonce, $cache_max_time, etc.) with no encapsulation; function has too many responsibilities (validation, config file writing, cron scheduling).
Code Quality: Direct mutation of $_POST values (e.g., '$_POST["cache_time_interval"] = 600') is a poor practice.
Security AUTO-FAIL: Security dimension scored below 5 due to absent capability check and unreliable nonce verification pattern.
